title: Chapter V.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: “While these are certain facts, since Avitianus related them to many persons, they are further confirmed on this ground that Refrigerius the presbyter, whom you see here present, lately had them narrated to him, under an appeal to the Divine majesty, by Dagridus, a faithful man among the tribunes, who swore that the account was given him by Avitianus himself.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But I do not wish you to wonder that I do to-day what I did not do yesterday; viz. that I subjoin to the mention of every individual wonder the names of witnesses, and mention persons to whom, if any one is inclined to disbelieve, he may have recourse, because they are still in the body.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: The unbelief of very many has compelled that; for they are said to hesitate about some things which were related yesterday.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Let these people, then, accept as witnesses persons who are still alive and well, and let them give more credit to such, inasmuch as they doubt our good faith.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But really, if they are so unbelieving, I give it as my opinion that they will not believe even the witnesses named.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: And yet I am surprised that any one, who has even the least sense of religion, can venture on such wickedness as to think that any one could tell lies concerning Martin.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Be that far from every one who lives in obedience to God; for, indeed, Martin does not require to be defended by falsehoods.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But, O Christ, we lay the truth of our whole discourse before thee, to the effect that we neither have said, nor will say, anything else than what either we ourselves have witnessed, or have learned from undoubted authorities, and, indeed, very frequently from Martin himself.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But although we have adopted the form of a dialogue, in order that the style might be varied to prevent weariness, still we affirm that we are really setting forth a true history in a dutiful spirit.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: The unbelief of some has compelled me, to my great regret, to insert in my narrative these remarks which are apart from the subject in hand.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But let the discourse now return to our assembly; in which since I saw that I was listened to so eagerly, I found it necessary to acknowledge that Aper acted properly in keeping back the unbelieving, under the conviction he had that those only ought to be allowed to hear who were of a believing spirit.
